--- SOURCE 1: Digital Religion as the Networked Spiritual Infrastructure of ... ---
URL: https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-93436-0_5

SUMMARY:
This chapter introduces the concept of digital religion, which asserts that to understand religion in contemporary culture, we must recognize that people engage in communal spiritual practices in both online-mediated spaces as well as through traditional offline communities and experiences. Those who study how religion takes place via Internet platforms have been particularly interested in how involvement in online communities may inform members’ understanding of what it means to be part of a religious group or church, especially how this may alter future expectations of how religious communities understand their faith offline (Hutchings, 2011. Working through these challenges and experimenting with how Internet technologies could provide solutions to the social separation encountered by their communities, religious leaders produced dozens of new expressions of religious practice, from digital worship services to online religious education, digital spiritual mentoring and counseling, Zoom meditation, and prayer gatherings.

# Digital Religion as the Networked Spiritual Infrastructure of Our Religious Futures

Digital Religion as the Networked Spiritual Infrastructure of Our Religious Futures

* [Heidi A. Campbell](#auth-Heidi_A_-Campbell) 
  [ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0001-7359-2033](https://orcid.org/0000-0001-7359-2033)[6](#Aff6)

Part of the book series:
[Management, Change, Strategy and Positive Leadership](https://link.springer.com/series/15772) ((MACHSTPOLE))

* 578 Accesses

## Abstract

This chapter introduces the concept of digital religion, which asserts that to understand religion in contemporary culture, we must recognize that people engage in communal spiritual practices in both online-mediated spaces as well as through traditional offline communities and experiences. I argue that our online and offline experiences of religion are deeply interconnected and force us to re-examine the nature of human spirituality in the twenty-first century. In this chapter, I discuss and define three key traits of digital religion: networked community, convergent practice, and multisite reality. Each of these traits suggests that the ways people participate in religious communities, rituals, and spiritual spaces have been altered by our engagement in a culture that is increasingly shaped by digital technologies. These three characteristics of digital religion became especially evident for religious groups during the global COVID-19 pandemic. They forced many congregations to move their religious services and practice online due to the social limits created by this health crisis. I assert that these new understandings about how individuals gather, practice their faith, and form spiritual relationships represent a unique and important shift in how religion should be conceived in Western culture.

## Introduction

As someone who has studied how religious groups have used and responded to Internet technologies since the 1990s, the global COVID-19 pandemic proved to be a pivotal time period for religious congregations adopting and adapting to digital technologies. I clearly remember logging onto Facebook on the first Sunday of the lockdown in the United States in March 2020. For the prior decade, I would often log on to Facebook on a Sunday morning and see about a half dozen friends and contacts streaming religious services they ran or attended on their Facebook profiles, inviting others to watch or worship with them. But that morning, my feed overflowed with over 30 live-streamed services running concurrently. One showed a large screenshot of a single Orthodox icon with the sound of a priest’s lone voice singing over it. A Lutheran priest stood alone in front of an altar of his church in Sweden leading prayers. A Pentecostal pastor in Mexico used a camera at the back of their church to try and capture their worship team, who were spread throughout their sanctuary, and an Episcopal pastor in the United States sat uncomfortably in his home study talking to his invisible congregation.

For the next 3 months, I tuned in each Sunday to observe the number of live-streamed religious services increase, and so I began to document what I have referred to in many talks on the impact of the pandemic on religion as the forced digital transition of religious congregations. The global COVID-19 pandemic left many religious leaders worldwide with only one option: either be willing to experiment with technology to keep their services going or lose connection with their congregations. Many pastors and priests also quickly discovered their congregations were victims of a digital divide, encountering a notable gap in their access to digital technologies and Internet access (Campbell, [2023](#ref-CR9 "Campbell, H. A. (2023). When churches discovered the digital divide: Overcoming technological inaccessibility, hesitancy & digital reluctance during the COVID-19 pandemic. Ecclesial Practices, 10(1), 36–61.")). It was not uncommon for many smaller churches to have no Wi-Fi set up in their buildings, let alone access to computers or digitally compatible audio equipment. March through May 2020 ushered in a large learning curve for religious congregations about how to best use and invest in information communication technology. Many churches had to rely on borrowed laptops, digital cameras, and even smartphones in those early days to try and figure out the best ways to build and connect to members of their congregation.

The forced digital transition of these congregations and other groups over the COVID-19 pandemic resulted in a huge uptake in digital media by religious communities as they tried to figure out the best ways to meet basic needs so they could continue their communication, programs, and connections during challenging times of social separation (Campbell & Osteen, [2021](#ref-CR11 "Campbell, H. A., & Osteen, S. (2021). When pastors put on the “tech hat”: How churches digitized during COVID-19. 
                  https://oaktrust.library.tamu.edu/handle/1969.1/194959
                  
                "); Holleman et al., [2022](#ref-CR17 "Holleman, A., Roso, J., & Chaves, M. (2022). Religious congregations’ technological and financial capacities on the eve of the COVID-19 pandemic. Review of Religious Research, 64, 163–188.")). In many respects, it was as if churches and other religious groups discovered the potential of Internet technologies to serve religious communities for the first time. This created a collective ah-ha moment for many religious leaders about how technology could empower the communal outreach and spiritual development of twenty-first-century congregations. While leaders sought to learn about and adopt technology quickly, they also realized they needed to quickly catch up on understanding a digital culture that had grown up around them, a culture that they had either not been willing to acknowledge or recognize. The pandemic did not create a dependency on technology. Still, in many respects, it did highlight how digital media has become a crucial part of our work, education, politics, and even family life (Bryson et al., [2020](#ref-CR5 "Bryson, J. R., Andres, L., & Davies, A. (2020). COVID-19, virtual church services and a new temporary geography of home. Tijdschrift voor economische en sociale geografie, 111(3), 360–372."); Chow & Kurlberg, [2020](#ref-CR14 "Chow, A., & Kurlberg, J. (2020). Two or three gathered online: Asian and European responses to COVID-19 and the digital church. Studies in World Christianity, 26(3), 298–318."); Dunlow, [2021](#ref-CR15 "Dunlow, J. (2021). Digital discipleship: A study of how churches in New York used technology for adult discipleship during the COVID-19 pandemic. Christian Education Journal, 18(3), 458–472.")). If this is the case, why not welcome it or at least consider bringing it into our religious lives?

Indeed, the use of digital media by religious groups and individuals to facilitate religious practices is in no way new or unique to the pandemic experience. In fact, over 25 years of research has been collected documenting how religious groups experiment with technology for spiritual purposes (see Campbell, [2013](#ref-CR8 "Campbell, H. A. (2013). Digital religion: Understanding religious practice in new media worlds. Routledge."); Campbell & Tsuria, [2021](#ref-CR12 "Campbell, H. A., & Tsuria, R. (2021). Digital religion: Understanding religious practice in digital culture (2nd ed.). Routledge.")). Yet, unique to the pandemic era was the wide-scale adoption and conscious integration of digital media into the life and work of many religious communities around the world. But rather than seeing this as a time of crisis that encouraged religious groups to innovate or cease to function for a season, I see the COVID-19 pandemic as a time of compulsory and unavoidable change many religious communities had been consciously avoiding. This moment required religious congregations to face the realities of life in the twenty-first century, a world that had already been wrapped in and driven by digital technology for many years. The pandemic forced religious communities to recognize our society, and religious communities are increasingly affected by digital culture (Lipková & Jarolímková, [2023](#ref-CR20 "Lipková, H., & Jarolímková, A. (2023). Twist in perception: Spiritual needs and technology in the times of COVID-19: A qualitative research study in the Czech Republic. Pastoral Psychology, 72(1), 143–167."); Ugboh, [2023](#ref-CR23 "Ugboh, G. (2023). The church and techno-theology: A paradigm shift of theology and theological practice to overcome technological disruptions. Journal of Ethics in Entrepreneurship and Technology, 3, 59–78.")).

## A World of Digital Religion

Living in a world that has been and continues to be shaped by digital media use requires religious communities to open their eyes and see how technology is, directly and indirectly, influencing the social practices and outlook of people in their congregations. Even if religious communities sought to avoid or ignore technology before the pandemic, it still informed many aspects of how their members communicated, worked, and developed spiritual disciplines in their everyday lives. Researchers like me have been documenting and investigating these trends for over two decades. Many aspects of digital technology shaping our current culture could be explored in this chapter, such as how digital practices have informed how we understand authority, identity, and authenticity in both secular and religious contexts (see Campbell & Bellar, [2023](#ref-CR10 "Campbell, H., & Bellar, W. (2023). Digital religion: The basics. Routledge.")). However, in order to focus the argument of this chapter, I limit my discussion to three prominent ways our spiritual practices and patterns have come to be informed by the digital tools and spaces in which we regularly interact. The social conditions and new form of interaction required during the COVID-19 pandemic spotlighted important ways that living in a digital culture has altered our understanding of religious community, ritual, and place. While these traits were made evident for most religious communities through their mediated experiences during the pandemic, they are in no way unique to the pandemic. I identified these tendencies over a decade ago in a research article published in *the Journal of the American Academy of Religion* and formed the basis of digital religion (Campbell, [2013](#ref-CR8 "Campbell, H. A. (2013). Digital religion: Understanding religious practice in new media worlds. Routledge.")).

Digital religion is a new conception of religion, which argues that in order to understand how religion is perceived and practiced in the twenty-first century, we must consider how offline or in-person religious practices intersect and are connected to online or digital religious practices. This is an understanding of religion, which I have identified and developed to better explain what contemporary religious practice looks like as it is engaged in both online and offline spaces. I argue that our online and offline experiences of religion are deeply interconnected, and our digital religious practices have come to inform our broader understanding of religion in society. In this chapter, I define and discuss the traits of networked community, convergent practice, and multisite reality and show how these characteristics of digital religion could be seen at work both before and during the COVID-19 pandemic.

## Networked Community

I argued that central to the idea of digital religion is the concept of networked community. This trait of networked community highlights the fact people increasingly build their social relationships in and through digital means. This form of network-based community building, in many ways, deviates from traditional forms of community. Now, we often create our social relationships through online spaces, such as forming a friendship around shared interests via an Instagram account or using a Facebook group to gather people who share a similar passion. So how people learn to *see* or interact with one another, and their social patterns become informed through the structures of networked technology (Wellman, [2001](#ref-CR24 "Wellman, B. (2001). Computer networks as social networks. Science, 293(5537), 2031–2034.")). Internet technology facilitates the building of what is referred to as networked interactions, social connections built through patterns of internetworking that privilege personal relational preferences over communal needs (Wellman, [2018](#ref-CR25 "Wellman, B. (2018). The network community: An introduction. In B. Wellman (Ed.), Networks in the global village (pp. 1–47). Routledge.")).

Since the early 1990s, researchers have been studying how people form and maintain relationships through Internet technology, which has come to be referred to as online communities. Scholars have shown that involvement in Internet-based groups often shapes their members’ broader conception of and participation in their offline communities. Those who study how religion takes place via Internet platforms have been particularly interested in how involvement in online communities may inform members’ understanding of what it means to be part of a religious group or church, especially how this may alter future expectations of how religious communities understand their faith offline (Hutchings, [2011](#ref-CR19 "Hutchings, T. (2011). Contemporary religious community and the online church. Information, Communication & Society, 14(8), 1118–1135.")). This is because online communities function quite differently than traditional religious institutions and structures. Rather than operating as tightly bounded groups, where social relationships are based on strong family ties, the limits of geography, and historic institutional affiliations, the online community is based on social connections driven by individual preferences and choices. This creates varying levels of affiliation and commitment. These loose social networks can be molded to the needs of individuals over groups. This concept of networked community suggests a fundamental change in how community is formed and maintained within online contexts, which has spilled over into how individuals conceive of community offline as well.

In the late 1990s, researchers began investigating this shifting understanding of the community they saw emerging within many secular and religious online groups. Early religious communities online involved people creating connections via email, discussion forums, or websites. For example, Muslims formed online communities to discuss issues of religious law via online fatwa websites. Buddhists developed new ways to conduct religious study or debate via cybersangha forums. Also, Jewish groups experimented with using early virtual reality (VR) and web space to create digital synagogues and prayer *minyans*. The Internet also allowed many spiritual teachers to offer classes and training, such as those offered by Spirituality & Practice, Center for Action and Contemplation, and later, many others. Scholars like me were interested in exploring individuals’ motivation for joining and investing time in these online communities. My early studies of online Christian communities showed that these groups did not see themselves as unconnected from offline Christianity, even though the traditional practices of prayer, worship, or spiritual development often needed to be modified when imported online.

Religious leaders have often voiced fear that involvement in an online religious community would undermine commitments to offline religious institutions and churches. Yet, studies like mine consistently demonstrated that online community typically served as a supplement, not substitute for offline religious involvement. Online religious community members often joined these online communities in order to meet specific relational needs, yet many recognized that these groups could not fully meet social needs and a desire for shared embodied worship experience. The new social structures and interactions that emerged within these online groups also require a new understanding of how contemporary religious groups function. It also highlighted the tendency for individuals to function within multiple social groups simultaneously, both online and offline.

These trends point toward a networked understanding of community, where people create specialized connections with groups that fit in with their spiritual needs and preferences, moving between involvement in different social groups so that they create a unique personalized network of relations. Online communities provide a space to easily build such personal social connections with others online and still affiliate with a broader or offline religious community. For many people during the pandemic, the idea of their social community being a network of self-directed relationships mediated via technology became a lived reality. Individuals could easily visit or become involved in multiple religious communities. For example, they may log on to an online platform like Second Life or the metaverse to attend an online Christian worship service and, afterward, find a website where they could practice meditation with another group through the use of avatars. This potential for creating individualized yet communal experiences online also expresses a networked form of community where the members can choose the depth of their involvement and affiliation.

Seeing and understanding a religious community as a social network is not limited to studies of religion online. Since the late 1990s, sociologists of religion have begun to note that offline religious communities increasingly employ social networking practices as they studied the patterns of some religious organizations. For example, Ammerman’s ([1997](#ref-CR2 "Ammerman, N. (1997). Congregation and community. Rutgers University Press.")) study of religious congregations saw many churches “functioning as a network” (p. 346). It asserted that “understanding of the social systems of modern life must start with the individual’s network of relations,” and thus, congregations should be approached as part of the social network of a community (p. 352). Using a social network analysis approach has also been noted in a number of studies of religious institutions and groups to assess levels of religious investment or degrees of social capital formed within these groups (Smith, [2003](#ref-CR22 "Smith, C. (2003). The secular revolution. University of California Press.")). For the past two decades, I have argued that the network metaphor provides a more accurate description of contemporary patterns of relationships, both online and offline. The network metaphor has become important for describing how people build, connect, and maintain their valued social relations online and offline, which are changeable, varying in depth, self-directed, and highly personalized. Community as a network provides an important new narrative for thinking about our religious interactions and spiritual relationships, especially coming out of the COVID-19 pandemic experience.

## Convergent Practice

Convergent practice emphasizes the fact that in the twenty-first century, many people now live out their spirituality by engaging in both online and offline forms of religious ritual, combining them into a personalized pattern of spirituality. Scholars of religion online have closely studied how spiritual traditions and religious rituals often must be adapted to fit within the technological structures and constraints of Internet platforms. For example, many spiritual practitioners and groups have had to think long and hard about how to transfer different embodied practices into disembodied digital spaces. Can an avatar performing prayer in an online temple in the VR space, such as the metaverse, be seen as an acceptable form of religious worship? Does gathering on a virtual meeting platform like Zoom for religious services count as a satisfactory way of performing one’s regular devotional practices? The debate about the authenticity or legitimacy of doing religious rituals online came to a head during the pandemic, with many communities deeming some forms of gathering online as an acceptable substitute, at least for a time. In contrast, other forms were deemed as not, such as the strong debates within Catholic and Orthodox communities about the theological limits of online Communion practices.

One of the other innate challenges posed by transferring religious practice online is that they were taken out of the communal, public context in which they were normally performed. They were taken out of an arena where they had a clear oversight of a recognized religious leader and placed within a personal and private context. Instead of sitting among one’s faith community and under the scrutiny of leaders during religious worship, the pandemic brought worship into private homes. This bringing of the sacred practice into mundane space meant the ritual was decontextualized, and for many, that meant it could be influenced by the culture of the home and the shape of technology. The trend of doing worship services online spawned hundreds of memes. Often depicted were families sitting on their living room couches watching worship services, usually in their pajamas or with a coffee in hand, turning this holy, active event into a passive spectacle. (For a discussion on the sacred, see Pargament and Pomerleau’s Chap. [4](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-93436-0_4) in this book.)

Yet even before the pandemic, research has noted that the culture of Internet searching and hyperlinks has already impacted how we seek information and conceive of facts and knowledge online. The Internet has made vast amounts of religious information accessible to community members and has taken private knowledge accessible only to religious community leaders and made it public. One of the byproducts of that is the flexibility of religious rituals online and fluid access to multiple sources of religious material can encourage individuals to blend aspects of rites based on information from new sources or experiences in different religious contexts. It can be argued that just like a networked community facilitates individualized forms of community, Internet platforms enable the faithful to build their own self-directed form of spiritual engagement online. Here, the Internet serves as a spiritual hub, allowing spiritual seekers to select from a vast array of resources and experiences to assemble and personalize their religious behavior and beliefs. This encourages a convergent form of religious practice online. Convergence is a term used to describe how digital media have brought together and combined traditional media technologies into a new form and in a new way. Digital media is a combination of telecommunication, broadcasting, and computer technologies linked through the digitization process. Scholars have also asserted that this technological convergence, seen clearly in the form and function of the Internet, is mirrored by a cultural convergence taking place in digital culture. The cultural convergence of digital media is that media audiences are no longer just consumers of content. Still, digital tools allow them to become cocreators of digital images and stories. This reshaping of the audience into a consumer and producer at the same time facilitates a process that allows and even encourages them to draw from multiple sources simultaneously. So, the technological landscape of the Internet removes many traditional barriers, allowing religious users new levels of freedom to create, access, and combine spiritual resources and practices that fit their own religious interpretations and experiences.

This increases the possibilities for how people interpret and express their religious lives online. For many, this means greater exposure to a myriad of spiritual beliefs, forms, and interpretations than they would otherwise have access to, both from within and without their religious tradition. This creates the possibility of creating new hybrids of traditional religions as different forms of knowledge and practice are combined to create individualized patterns of spiritual life. The Internet thus becomes, for many, a religious marketplace encouraging users to seek out preferred information or establish personalized practices rather than simply link to an official religious institution or mirror set protocols like they might have done in the past.

This tendency for religious Internet users to engage in convergent practices has been well documented. Initial investigations found early religious adopters of the Internet readily embraced the Internet as a sacred space in order to both import traditional rituals online and create new forms of religiosity online. Researchers have documented the adoption and adaptation of various religious forms in online environments, such as Hindus and Buddhists engaging in chanting and meditation in front of specially designed cyber-altars or shrines online meant to replicate holy sites from their country of origin so they can take place in virtual ceremonies during religious festivals. Helland ([2008](#ref-CR16 "Helland, C. (2008). Online religion as lived religion: Methodological issues in the study of religious participation on the internet. Heidelberg Journal of Religions on the Internet. 
                  https://doi.org/10.11588/rel.2005.1.380
                  
                ")) argued the very structure of the Internet supports and even encourages the emergence of diverse religious traditions. Importing traditional religious rituals and artifacts online means religious seekers have the opportunity to reinterpret the context and use, thereby transforming traditional practice in ways that may challenge offline ecclesiastical organizations. The Internet allows many religious devotees access to information and sources of interpretation that were once exclusively accessible to learned or high-ranking religious leaders. This can allow members to bypass traditional gatekeepers of religious knowledge (Barker, [2005](#ref-CR3 "Barker, E. (2005). Crossing the boundary: New challenges to religious authority and control as a consequence of access to the internet. In M. T. Hojsgaard & M. Warburg (Eds.), Religion and cyberspace (pp. 67–85). Routledge.")).

The fluidity of hypertexts and hyperlinks also creates new possibilities for spiritual pilgrimage and linking different ideas and concepts. As the Internet empowers religious seekers with a certain degree of autonomy and as more religious information becomes available online, it facilitates new freedom in the acquisition of ritual knowledge that may bypass normally required initiation rites. This encourages the personal pursuit of religious knowledge that will benefit the chosen life practice of the individual religious practitioner. Because this religious practice is taking place within an online religious group, it may not be so much about the collective performance of a set of rituals but about the freedom of collective reinterpretation. In this way, an online group can become constituted as a conversational community, allowing individuals to practice their faith in very individualized ways instead of being tied to the boundaries or constraints of a specific religious tradition. Therefore, through sharing new ideas and reinterpretations of symbols related to religious practice, an experiential environment is created, allowing the group to be together yet separately live out their spiritualities. Multiple examples of online practices that exhibit traits of convergent practices can be found from the adaptation of traditional forms of worship in virtual temples, such as the practice of online puja, to the creation of online rituals that combine traditional and personalized interpretations of accepted religious pr

--- SOURCE 2: Embracing Digital Worship ---
URL: https://www.numberanalytics.com/blog/embracing-digital-worship

SUMMARY:
Digital worship has emerged as a significant phenomenon, allowing people to connect with their spiritual communities and engage in religious practices online.

--- SOURCE 3: Religion and the internet: digital religion, (hyper)mediated ... ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/

SUMMARY:
While in this article I still employ the terms “online” and “offline” to describe practices that are connected to the Internet or to physical spaces, I embrace the idea of thinking of religious practices as increasingly “onlife.” Rethinking the terminologies that describe digital religion also helps to account for the contemporary high-tech society where many interpersonal communications occur through digital media, including religious experiences. While some hypermediated practices might disappear when social and physical distancing rules are no longer in place, the intensification of Internet connections during moments of lockdown likely created the conditions to think of space, materiality, and digital religion in new ways.

Religion and the internet: digital religion, (hyper)mediated spaces, and materiality
====================================================================================

Religion und Internet: Digitale Religion, (hyper)mediatisierte Räume und Materialität
=====================================================================================

[Giulia Evolvi](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Evolvi%20G%22%5BAuthor%5D)

### Giulia Evolvi

PMCID: PMC8525051 PMID: [34938947](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34938947/)

[](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/)Abstract
-------------------------------------------------------------

This article offers theoretical reflections on the study of religion and the Internet by critically discussing the notion of “digital religion” (Campbell 2012). In particular, it stresses the importance of integrating material and spatial approaches to the study of digital religion. In doing so, it proposes the theory of “hypermediated religious spaces” to describe processes of religious mediation between online and offline environments by taking into account materiality and space. The article discusses theoretical perspectives by means of case studies: first, the importance of materiality within Internet practices is illustrated through the example of Neo-Pagan online rituals; second, the notion of space, and “third space” in particular, in relation to Internet practices is analyzed through the case of the hashtag #Nous-Sommes-Unis, circulated by French Muslims; third, the theory of hypermediated spaces is exemplified by the analysis of a live-streamed mass in the Italian city of Manerbio during the Covid-19 lockdown. The article aims at kindling scholarly reflections on terminologies and theories for the global and interdisciplinary study of digital religion.

**Keywords:** Digital Religion, Space, Materiality, Hypermediated Spaces, Internet

[](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/)Introduction
-----------------------------------------------------------------

The image of Pope Francis standing alone in a dark and empty St Peter’s square on the 27th of March 2020, giving a special _Urbi et Orbi_ blessing to pray for the end of the Covid-19 pandemic (Watkins [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR56)), exemplifies some facets of religion during the Covid-19 lockdown. The blessing is symbolically important as it occurred when the world was struggling with new lockdown restrictions, particularly severe in Italy. Arguably, the image is remarkable because of the emptiness of St. Peter’s square, a space usually full of believers. As people could no longer physically participate in religious functions, the special _Urbi et Orbi_ was consumed exclusively through media, either television or Internet streaming.

This image may suggest that the Covid lockdown brought extraordinary changes to religion, with mediation substituting the physicality of believers gathered around the pontiff. Indeed, during the Covid-19 lockdown several religious communities around the world were forced to find online strategies to substitute physical interactions (Campbell [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR10)a). However, two considerations need to be done. First, as media scholar Stewart Hoover ([2006](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR27)) asserts, religion and media have always been entangled throughout history. Religion is inevitably based on messages diffused through media, from sacred texts to new media technologies (Horsfield [2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR28)). The second consideration is that the reproduction of religious rituals online is not something that is only caused by physical distancing restrictions, and it is not limited to social network sites (Hadden and Cowan [2000](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR21)). For instance, Pope John Paul II already spoke favorably about the possibility of diffusing Catholic messages online at the World Communication Day in 1990 (Jonh Paul II [1990](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR32)). Since then, some religious communities from all traditions chose to partially or completely move their activities to the Internet, creating the phenomenon of “online churches” (Hutchings [2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR30)).

Hence, the possibility of participating in the Pope’s mass through media technology is not something new or extraordinary. However, an image such as that of Pope Francis preaching alone in St Peter’s square is arguably impactful because it makes people aware of the absence of physicality during the function and the empty space around the pontiff. The intensification of online mediation during the Covid-19 pandemic urges scholars to further reflect upon questions they have already asked for decades: how does mediation affect religious communities, identities, and authorities? How do people recreate a sense of materiality through digital technologies? How are spaces of religious practice negotiated online? With these questions as starting point, the aim of this article is to present theoretical reflections on the notion of “virtual environment” in relation to religion. Concerning terminology, in the article I will predominantly use the term “space” instead of “environment.” This is because I draw from theories that define space as the result of material and mental practices (Knott [2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR34)), and help me contextualize space as connected to the Internet. In the first part of the article, I will explain that online and offline spaces are entangled in contemporary religious practices by presenting the theoretical framework of “digital religion” (Campbell [2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR9)). Then, I will discuss the notion of materiality in relation to the theory of mediation (Meyer [2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR42)). I will also offer reflections on the notion of space and the Internet, discussing the spatial turn in religious studies and the notion of third spaces of digital religion (Hoover and Echchaibi [2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR26)). Lastly, I will discuss the theory of hypermediation (Scolari [2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR52)) to describe the fluid interactions between different media platforms and physical spaces. It is important to notice that materiality and space are increasingly incorporated in studies of technologies, media, and related fields (see, for instance, Herzogenrath [2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR23)), so this article is connected to larger scholarly debates on these topics.

While the scope of the article is mainly theoretical, I will illustrate my claims employing some case studies about different religious traditions. In specific, I will describe Neo-Pagan online rituals to explore how digital religion employs materiality to enhance a sense of communal participation. I will also analyze the diffusion of the hashtag #NousSommesUnis (We Are United), spread by French Muslims in the aftermath of the 2015 terrorist attacks in Paris, as an example of how digital religion exists in spaces across different platforms. Then, I will analyze the case of a Catholic church in Italy employing live streaming as a substitute for physical engagement during the Covid-19 lockdown to show how materiality and space are hypermediated during moments of limited physical interactions. This last case is the only one addressing religion during the Covid-19 pandemic, and aims both at highlighting how this historical moment intensified online practices, and at stressing continuities with previous examples of digital religion. These case studies are based on my own research and present data acquired through qualitative interviews and qualitative content analysis of media texts. Establishing whether online religious functions and interactions are authentic is beyond the scope of this article, but it aims at offering an overview of theories on materiality, space, and religion that will hopefully help understand online and offline performances as entangled rather than separated.

[](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/)Digital religion: online and offline practices
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Because religious groups have employed the Internet since the 1990s, this is also the period when scholars started analyzing empirical cases of religion in Internet venues. While it is a relatively recent field of study, the exploration of religion and the Internet already underwent four waves of research (Hojsgaard and Warburg [2005](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR25); Lövheim and Campbell [2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR39)). The first wave, which took place in the 1990s, tends to describe the Internet as a space that is separate from physical reality, and that invites practices unrelated to the offline dimension. This is why scholars employed terms such as “cyberspace” and “cyber-religion” (Brasher [2004](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR5)), which indicated the Interned as a space “other” than everyday activities. During the second wave of studies on religion and the Internet, scholars started to approach digital spaces in a more nuanced way. In the early 2000s, Christopher Helland ([2000](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR22)) introduced the distinction between “religion online,” indicating established religious groups that use the Internet to enhance their activities, and “online religion,” describing interactive online practices existing mostly, or exclusively, online. This distinction is important because, first, it stresses the existence of religious groups that simultaneously occupy online spaces and offline—material—spaces. Second, it highlights how the Internet can create new types of practices that are intrinsically different from those occurring without the aid of digital technologies.

The third and fourth waves are characterized by the theoretical approach of “digital religion.” Differently from cyber-religion or online religion, digital religion entails that online and offline spaces are entangled. Heidi Campbell ([2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR9)) describes digital religion as:

> [A]rticulating the evolution of religious practice online, as seen in the most recent manifestations of cyberchurches, which are linked to online and offline contexts simultaneously. “Digital religion” does not simply refer to religion as it is performed and articulated online, but points to how digital media and spaces are shaping and being shaped by religious practice.

Hence, a digital religion approach, which I also assume in this article, considers that there is no clear distinction between religious actions that exist online and those that occur offline. This does not mean that the online and offline spheres are identical, and that people do not distinguish between them, but that the Internet is increasingly entangled with everyday activities. The aforementioned example of Pope Francis preaching in St Peter’s square during the Covid-19 lockdown can be considered an instance of digital religion as it shows how an offline space can be mediated to enhance offline practices. Believers would have been unable to listen and watch Pope Francis doing the _Urbi et Orbi_ blessing without media technologies, but at the same time the ritual is based on a material space. Therefore, this shows how media technology creates new conditions for the practice of religion where materiality and space do not disappear, but are present in new forms.

Because of the entanglement of physical and virtual actions that characterize digital religion, the terms “online” and “offline” may not always offer a helpful theoretical distinction. Regarding digital practices, philosopher Luciano Floridi ([2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR19)) proposes the term “onlife” as a substitute for the online-offline dichotomy. The notion of onlife indicates the blurring of boundaries between real and virtual, as well as of the distinction between human, machine, and nature. While in this article I still employ the terms “online” and “offline” to describe practices that are connected to the Internet or to physical spaces, I embrace the idea of thinking of religious practices as increasingly “onlife.” Rethinking the terminologies that describe digital religion also helps to account for the contemporary high-tech society where many interpersonal communications occur through digital media, including religious experiences.

However, while contemporary religion largely occurs in contexts of constant mediation, there are two important considerations that need to be done. First, most works analyzing examples of religion and media, including the case studies I present in this article, focus on contexts, such as the North American or European ones, with a high presence of digital media (Hjarvard and Lovheim [2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR24)). Scholars also need to consider that there are locations where Internet connection is scarcer, and that the condition of being onlife depends on economic possibility, access to technology, media education, and skills. Second, while media proliferate in several contexts, there are religious communities that make the conscious choice of not engaging with technology. The work of Campbell ([2007](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR8)) on the so-called “kosher phone” describes how certain ultra-orthodox Jewish communities in Israel work together with developers to create smartphones that only connect to sites deemed appropriate by religious authorities. This is an example of how religious groups can consciously negotiate the boundaries between online and offline spaces even in highly mediated contexts. Religious communities’ agency in employing new technologies has also been explored by Campbell with the notion of “spiritualizing the Internet,” an approach that frames the Internet as suitable for religious engagements (Campbell [2005](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR7), p.2). This theoretical standpoint is further elaborated in analyzing how religious people create meaning through media narratives, in what Campbell defines “technological apologetic” (Campbell [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR11)b).

Therefore, based on Campbell’s approaches, I propose to think about digital religion not as a given, but as a process where religious groups and individuals adopt—and adapt to—digital technologies depending on their values and possibilities. The case of Pope Francis preaching during the Covid-19 lockdown is an example of how religion can—and must—employ digital technologies in situations that offer few alternatives. To explore the entanglements between online and offline venues, it is relevant to analyze two interconnected elements of religious practices: materiality and space.

[](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/)Digital religion and materiality
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Conceptualizing digital religion as connected to physical experiences helps to think about Internet practices as material. Hence, the Internet is not an immaterial environment “other” than the material reality, but it is linked to practices that exist in material spaces. It is for this reason that, in this section, I will explain the importance of materiality for the theory of religious mediation, and I will apply it to the case study of Neo-Pagans performing online rituals.

Concerning materiality, Keenan and Arweck ([2006](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR33)) write that “The idea of religion itself is largely intelligible outside its incarnation in material expressions” (pp. 2–3). This approach marks a material turn that rejects the dualism between mind and body, and considers art, music, spatial practices, clothing, and literature as integral parts of the religious experience. According to David Morgan ([2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR44)), material practices are not secondary aspects, but they are the very core of religion. Examples of religious materiality include the Muslim female veil, which does not only indicate religious belonging, but symbolizes complex negotiations in terms of ethnic identity and status (Ünal and Moors [2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR55)). Besides, Valentina Napolitano ([2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR45)) analyzes the act of Pope Francis to show a wooden cross on the Italian island of Lampedusa, where several migrants arrived in dinghy boats from North Africa. The cross, made of wood from shipwrecked boats, became a powerful material symbol employed to unite people and emotionally move them.

The role of objects in everyday religious experiences suggests that materiality is involved in processes of religious mediation that help frame relations among people and diffuse religious messages. In other words, objects constitute what anthropologist Birgit Meyer ([2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR42)) describes as “sensational forms,” which are practices and attitudes that help structure the transcendental. Hence, for Meyer ([2006](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR41)) objects allow people to experience religion by materializing it:

> Posing a distance between human beings and the transcendental, religion offers practices of mediation that bridge that distance and makes it possible to experience—and from a more distanced perspective of could say: produce—the transcendental. (p.13)

Meyer explains mediation with the example of the Catholic icon: even if it is human-made, it is believed to embody the sacred presence, and it can be experienced with the senses, through a gaze, a prayer, or a kiss (_ibidem_). Mediation, a theory elaborated also in relation to media consumption (Martin-Barbero [1993](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR40)), is here conceptualized as a process that brings believers from immanence to transcendence through religious media. The term “media,” in this context, designates all the objects, performances, and pieces of art that participate in the religious experience, and includes audiovisual media, studied by Meyer in Pentecostal-charismatic churches in Ghana. Drawing, among others, from the work of Meyer, Arjun Appadurai ([2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR1)) situates the relationship between mediation and materiality beyond the study of religion, describing as “mediants” all the human and non-human actors that participate in material practices, such as those connected to housing and shelter.

Drawing from these theories of mediation, I propose there are three main reasons to approach digital religion considering materiality. First, digital artifacts and technological devices—smartphones, tablets, computers—are themselves material objects that can embody the religious experience (Campbell and Connelly [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR12)). Second, digital media may not allow tactile practices, but they facilitate visual culture which, Morgan ([2005](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR43)) writes, also helps to materialize religion by providing a“sacred gaze” connected to the sensors consumption of images. Third, there are practices that help “rematerializing” the digital. As Tim Hutchings ([2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR29)) writes, the materiality of the Internet lies in the different affordances of the platforms—including design, aesthetics, coding, and interfaces—as well as in the actions and relations that digital practices make possible. Hutchings employs various examples to illustrate this approach: applications that create the possibility of reading the Bible on a technological device and connect with other believers, printed Bibles linked to online videos to enhance the reading experience, and graves with QR codes that permit people to grieve their loved ones with the help of dedicated websites. These case studies show how materiality can be embedded in Internet practices that activate relationships and identities.

Therefore, it is helpful to think of digital media in material terms because they may help people relate with the transcendental in practices of mediation, they include material technologies and visual aesthetics, and they are embedded in everyday material practices. The case of online rituals can illustrate how materiality and mediation are connected: in exploring Buddhist meditation in virtual environments, Heidi Campbell and Louise Connelly ([2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR12)) highlight how people meditate through avatars and digital artifacts that reproduce material objects, while at the same time reproducing practices in the offline space. This is also true for Neo-Paganism, an umbrella term that includes several religious communities that draw from pre-Christian beliefs. These groups share various characteristics and usually accord great importance to rituals and community practices, which they adapted to online environments already in the 1990s (Grieve [1995](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR20)). While not all Neo-Pagans employ digital technologies, many rely on the Internet to get in contact with like-minded people and create online communities (Renser and Tiidenberg [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR49)). Douglas Cowan ([2005](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR14)) discusses Neo-Pagan online rituals as creating a placeness experience, and emphasizes that an Internet-mediated experience cannot replicate the sensorial practices that people are supposed to activate in a physical location. However, in my previous work (Evolvi [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8525051/#CR18)) I argued that Neo-Pagan online engagement, while indubitably different than an offline ritual, is also characterized by material expressions.

An example of how Neo-Pagans embed materiality in online rituals can be found on the website JaguarMoon ([www.jaguarmoon.org](http://www.jaguarmoon.org/)). Based in the U.S., JaguarMoon is a cyber coven that offers online classes and creates environments for online rituals. I interviewed the founder of the website, and we discussed the peculiarities of online rituals, which are mainly held because Neo-Pagans often do not find many other practitioners in their homeplaces, or they fear the social stigma of disclosing their religion. She explained that online rituals were initially held through chatrooms, and then they started to employ software that allowed people to speak and see each other. According to my interviewee, the principle of online rituals is to focus on visualization and connect with other practitioners through the computer to rise energy. She describes online rituals as follows:

> In a physical group when everybody is right in a room, you’re all connected, energy raises the air of your arms, back on your neck, you’re based in it. Online rituals, since you’re alone in a room, really depend on your ability of visualization and to literally imagine yourself connected with other people in the astral. But as far as I’m concerned cyberspace is astral, is a different portion of that plane so you are making an energetic connection with the priestess and the leader, what I do when I’m doing ritual is that I visualize a connection being made with all different people around physically (personal communication, 17 September 2016)

In this quote, the interviewee described the importance of visualization to give a sense of physicality and materiality to an online ritual. In the interview, she also explained some strategies to embed material practices in online rituals, such as creating altars and placing candles near the computer. For example, she described a ritual of abundance where people are encouraged to take an egg, paint some symbols on it with cinnamon and vegetable oil while in front of the computer, and eat it the day after. This practice helps people to feel the material part of the ritual while at the same time being connected with others through the Internet. Materiality is embedded in various ways: the computer is a material object that makes people feel connected with each other, tangible objects such as eggs and candles help believers recreate a sense of materiality around the computer, and imagination allows the participants to visualize the ritual and raise the energy as a community. The interviewee, as per the aforementioned quote, considered cyberspace

# CAHSS Professor on How Evolving Technology is Transforming Religions in a Digital World

Back to Article Listing

### Author(s)

#### Susan Dugan

Writer

## 

Feature  •

[Research & Scholarship](/news-events/all-articles/category/research-&-scholarship)
 •

*Members of religious institutions across denominations around the world*[*are increasingly turning to websites, apps, social media and AI for religious study, worship and practice*](https://www.pewresearch.org/religion/2023/06/02/use-of-apps-and-websites-in-religious-life/)*. Assistant Professor of Hindu Studies, Critical Theory and Digital Religion*[*Dheepa Sundaram*](https://liberalarts.du.edu/about/people/dheepa-sundaram) *researches and teaches on religious practices in digital spaces, diving deep into “digital meaning making and the making of digital religious publics, particularly in South-Asian contexts.” Her research also examines extremism in various digital platforms and interfaces and the formation of religious nationalist publics online.*

*According to*[*Sandra Dixon*](https://liberalarts.du.edu/about/people/sandra-lee-dixon)*, chair,*[*Department of Religious Studies*](https://liberalarts.du.edu/religious-studies "https://liberalarts.du.edu/religious-studies")*,*[*College of Arts, Humanities & Social Sciences*](https://liberalarts.du.edu/ "https://liberalarts.du.edu/")*(CAHSS), Sundaram’s research, publications and teaching about digital technologies and religious practice are raising awareness about how religious practices adapt to and affect social, cultural, economic and political environments.*

*“Her writing alerts us to the intersection of religious and political factors, so that we pay attention when Jews and Palestinian Muslims connect on social media to contest and survive events in Israel and Gaza, sometimes within their own religious group and sometimes through interfaith communication. The public and the press need to recognize that religion does not sit apart while technology takes over the rest of the world. Religious practice through digital media is happening now and Professor Sundaram helps explore its global social and political impact.”*

*CAHSS News asked Sundaram to share her insights into how technology is reshaping the way we think about and access religion and spiritual practice, the pros and cons, the problem of religious disinformation and what the future might hold.*

#### **Tell us about your work and the classes you teach.**

Most of my research work focuses on social media. I look at Facebook hate groups, for example, but also at how religions intersect with newer emerging technologies and virtual reality. Right now, I’m working on an AI project focused on digital labor and capitalism.  

I teach an undergraduate/graduate course called “Surfing for Salvation” which is a take on one of the first articles that came out on digital religion in 2002 by Christopher Helland. I take a critical-theory approach, require students to build a website for assignments and create their own personal intellectual brand. The idea is to experience how narrative building and web design are not neutral. Religious digital spaces are a way of remaking that religious practice.

Some of my students have looked at online engagement with digital scriptures. One of my former students had an interesting project about how people were engaging in Instagram accounts that had spiritual components and were also anti-suicide, presenting scripture in friendly, welcoming, introspective ways for young people who felt marginalized from their religions.

#### **How** ***is*****technology remaking religion in digital spaces?**

What we call digital religion — non-analog engagement with religion, spirituality, faith, cultural communities — starts around 1999 when, for example, in the Hindu context, you see the earliest ritual website where you can pay somebody in India to perform a ritual for you. Those kinds of websites along with online churches have been around a long time.

With the advent of Facebook in 2004 and Instagram in 2012 you started seeing more streaming and recording of religious services and a shift toward building community. Those digital engagements developed into a kind of robust hybrid engagement with spiritualities and cultural practices online and offline, digital spaces as an extension of what church means to people.

#### **Which demographic groups are more or less likely to use technology to access religion?**

We see the highest engagement across all religious digital platforms, interfaces, apps, videos and social media in the under-age-30 group. We also see high engagement in people over age 60 who really enjoy accessing social media coverage of religious events on platforms like Facebook. Facebook allows older folks to share long-form posts, pictures and videos and connect as part of a broader community, whereas younger people would often rather just watch a video on YouTube, maybe clip it and put it on TikTok and then scroll TikTok.

Churches, synagogues, temples and mosques understand — perhaps better than our own government — how different social media platforms appeal to different demographics. They also understand that a robust website that allows people to access information and interact easily can increase donations.

#### **Which religious groups are more or less likely to engage technology for religious purposes?**

It really depends on the country and the community. For example, diaspora communities of Indians in the United States often feel isolated from the homeland and are more likely to frequent in-person religious spaces for worship, whereas in India you see higher online engagement.

In the U.S. you see much more digital engagement in Christian spaces. But across religions there is always this tension between extending access and broadening congregation versus adherence to certain perceived traditional methods of establishing authority and authentic religious practice. For example, Hindus believe that unless a ritual is performed in a consecrated temple in the land of India, there is no karmic benefit or “result” from doing the ritual.

We similarly hear some people in Christian churches that congregation means you must be in a physical building together. And Islam makes this case that coming together online for prayers is not what the Quran intended. These tensions came to the fore during COVID.

> ### *Until social media and web searches no longer favor engagement over content as the main metric driving algorithmic information, we are trapped in a world in which misinformation and disinformation impact religions as well as everything else.*

#### **What are the biggest pros and cons of this trend?**

The biggest pros are providing greater access and opportunities to rethink things like what constitutes sacred space and how we understand religious authenticity and authority. It also democratizes knowledge, which is a good thing.

As a con, extremist groups of all types — not just religious — are already using AI chat and text generators to create fake profiles of people who they deem to be enemies, putting out information that people take as true about people who they don’t like, including myself. Because of what I do, I have personally experienced this kind of hate.

#### **Does technology make it easier to spread religious misinformation and disinformation online?**

Yes. In digital spaces the mechanisms of power that control knowledge production are capital-based and determined by who has the most likes. If you have a million dollars and thousands and thousands of likes on the post, it tells the algorithm that every person who saw that wants more of that. If you have a large digital platform and are sharing what you see as tenets of a particular religious tradition, people may take that as trusted information if they trust the group they learned it in and the person who shared it. 

Social networks are built on trust. It is not unusual to trust a person who you know and like on a social media platform over a person who has studied that tradition, a researched book on that tradition or an authorized figure like a priest, pastor or rabbi. To this point, folks are more likely to believe something someone in their local church website group posts without checking the authenticity, and that’s how misinformation and disinformation spread online. Until social media and web searches no longer favor engagement over content as the main metric driving algorithmic information, we are trapped in a world in which misinformation and disinformation impact religions as well as everything else.

#### **What is most exciting and most worrisome for you personally about how technology is transforming religions?**

I’m excited that technology is providing new ways for people to learn about, practice and connect with their religions, but terrified about the impact of AI-generated sacred texts. I fear we will see sacred texts end up in an information marketplace where one version may be different than another, which is completely normal. However, we may eventually lose our ability to determine the authenticity and authority of sacred texts through traditional research methods. Religious textual authority will likely be determined by market dynamics such as engagement, downloads and popularity. This is already happening.

As a person who also reads traditional sacred texts in primary languages, I fear we may end up in a situation where a bad actor can just put out anything, resulting in people mistaking problematic views for what is in the text. Eventually nobody will be able to verify what physical manuscripts of sacred texts actually say. Without the practice of reading and engaging with manuscripts and sacred texts in their original languages, we can’t engage in the kind of research that we value as scholars. This will shape how lived traditions and canons are articulated and understood in the digital age in ways for which we are, perhaps, not yet prepared.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital platforms have facilitated a variety of worship experiences, from live-streamed services to virtual prayer groups, enabling congregations to maintain spiritual connections despite physical distance.

Social media influencers have become pivotal in shaping religious narratives online, engaging younger audiences and creating communities around shared spiritual practices and values across diverse platforms.

Digital religion increasingly incorporates mental health resources, with many religious communities offering online counseling and support groups, addressing the holistic needs of their members during challenging times.
